message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Decisions about the continued need for control measures and the effect of introducing COVID-19 vaccinations rely on accurate and population-based data on SARS-CoV-2 positivity and risk factors for testing positive.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this interrupted time series of population-based nationwide cross-sectional studies, data from nasopharyngeal testing and questionnaires were used to estimate the SARS-CoV-2 RNA prevalence and factors associated with test positivity over the 1
                    <jats:sup>st</jats:sup>
                    year of the COVID-19 epidemic.
                  </jats:p>
                  <jats:p>
                    The study is registered with the ISRCTN Registry,
                    <jats:ext-link xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="isrctn" xlink:href="10182320">ISRCTN10182320</jats:ext-link>
                    .
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Between April 23, 2020 and February 2, 2021, results were available from 34,915 individuals and 27,870 samples from 11 consecutive studies. The percentage of people testing positive for SARS-CoV-2 decreased from 0.27% (95% CI 0.10% - 0.59%) in April to 0.04% (95% CI 0.00% - 0.22%) by the end of May and remained very low (0.01%, 95% CI 0.00% - 0.17%) until the end of August, followed by an increase since November (0.37%, 95% CI 0.18% - 0.68%) that escalated to 2.69% (95% CI 2.08% - 2.69%) in January 2021. In addition to substantial change in time, an increasing number of household members (for one additional OR 1.15, 95% CI 1.02-1.29), reporting current symptoms of COVID-19 (OR 2.21, 95% CI 1.59-3.09), and completing questionnaire in the Russian language (OR 1.85, 95% CI 1.15-2.99) were associated with increased odds for SARS-CoV-2 RNA positivity.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>SARS-CoV-2 population prevalence needs to be carefully monitored as vaccine programmes are rolled out in order to inform containment decisions.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Our study relies upon nation-wide and population-based data on SARS-CoV-2 prevalence, and presents changes in prevalence over the whole 1st year of the Covid-19 epidemic.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Our analysis of SARS-CoV-2 infection risk factors is not limited to notification or health care-based case data.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Selection bias may have been introduced as a result of low response rate. The direction of bias is unclear, but most likely operates rather uniformly over the period of observation, though this presents less of a threat to the SARS-CoV-2 prevalence trend analysis.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Our data could be used to adequately project the future course of the SARS-CoV-2 epidemic and the effect of control measures.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: The 1st year of the COVID-19 epidemic in Estonia: an interrupted time series of population based nationwide cross-sectional studies
